entry.title: My last Eye on Boise post
Recent law leads to ‘delicate’ navigation of ivermectin sales...
Idaho land board opts to use attorneys outside Idaho AG's office for most legal services...
Feds approve Idaho's request to ban candy, soda purchases using SNAP...
Trump endorses Idaho Gov. Brad Little for 2026 election...
Refugee farmers front and center at new West Bench Farmers Market...
In this rural Idaho county, paramedics are being trained to help with mental health crises...
On D-Day, veterans rally against VA cuts, Trump...
Possible mifepristone restrictions raise alarm for some in Idaho, nationwide...
Contrasting Boise, city of Nampa doubles down on Idaho flag law...
IDHW director stresses rural health funding has broad eligibility as grant opportunities arrive
Longtime Idaho state employee takes over as director of ITD
Former Idaho Supreme Court Justice John Stegner launches gubernatorial campaign
Boise woman's federal arson trial continued until Nov. 2
An Idaho mother who said her toddler twins died after vaccinations has been charged with murder
Abortion-rights measure moves closer to Idaho ballot
Trump approves Idaho disaster declaration for North Idaho storm
Idaho National Laboratory helps World Cup cities prepare for disaster
Idaho judge dismisses defamation suit brought by Michael Boren over ‘neglect’ of case
‘Still an evolving experiment’: Josh Ritter reflects on Idaho ties, hints at July 4 performance
Community weighs in on blueprint for a potential future Caldwell
LEAP's newest project introduces affordable housing for seniors
Developer partnering with Coeur d'Alene Tribe on project aiming to shift Treasure Valley's border eastward
Boise Centre considers River Street expansion
Micron milestone: Memory chip maker reaches $1 trillion market cap
Terrace Drive-In will open this summer after all
Mini-subdivision approved in Traverse Creek Subdivision in Caldwell
Gem In & Out thrives as longest-standing restaurant in Caldwell
New outdoor event venue fledges in Boise this summer
Bargains abound with new grocer coming to town
Developer partnering with Coeur d'Alene Tribe on project aiming to shift Treasure Valley's border eastward
entry.summary: Thousands attended the 60th God and Country Festival in Namp… At 19, Joshua Ritter, born in Moscow to parents Robert and S… The Peregrine will be arriving in Elmore County near two planned residential communities and a future Sho-Pai Casino and Resort. BOISE — Meridian-based developer Ahlquist and the Coeur d’Alene Tribe have announced the Peregrine, a future, mixed-use development that will push the border of the Treasure Valley eastward as residential development in the area continues forward. The 920-acre mixed-use project will be located in Elmore County and in close proximity to both the Boise Airport and Micron’s Boise campus. The development will offer industrial, commercial, service, retail and residential uses and is set to feature a 774-acre industrial and technology park as well as a 146-acre commercial park. The park is currently available for sale or lease and is intending to create a “new eastern border of the Treasure Valley,” according to a press release from the Coeur d’Alene Tribe and Ahlquist, who have formed a partnership to bring the development forward. “Ahlquist is honored to partner with the Coeur d’Alene Tribe on a project of this magnitude,” Ahlquist CEO Tommy Ahlquist said in the release. “As the eastern edge of the Treasure Valley continues to grow, we believe Peregrine will play an important role in shaping the future of this corridor.” The site located off Interstate 84 at the Simco Road interchange will be across from two planned residential communities in Mayfield, Idaho, that are expected to bring more than 10,000 homes to the area. The development will also sit close to the future Sho-Pai Resort and Casino, which is similarly being developed in partnership with the Coeur d’Alene Tribe, the release said. A rendering of the 920-acre Peregrine development. The mixed-use project is the product of a partnership with Ahlquist and the Coeur d''Alene Tribe and will feature a commercial park as well as an industrial and technology park. “A project of this size and ambition reflects the Coeur d’Alene Tribe’s long-standing commitment to thoughtful, lasting economic development, and we are excited to help bring it to the Treasure Valley,” Chief Allan, chairman of the Coeur d’Alene Tribe, said. “This development is an opportunity to meet the needs of Idaho business owners, while also bringing new vitality to the area where we are in the process of helping the Shoshone-Paiute Tribes build their first and only resort and casino.” The development announcement arrives as Micron’s Boise campus, located 12 minutes from the mixed-use site, has expanded significantly in recent years alongside the artificial intelligence boom. Last year, in a joint announcement with the Trump administration, Micron announced a $200 billion expansion across the U.S., including $30 billion for Boise’s second memory fabrication plant. “Micron’s investment in Idaho will drive transformational economic growth and create tremendous opportunities across the region,” Ahlquist said in the release. We’re proud to provide a location that can support and accommodate that expansion.” The Boise Airport, approximately 15 minutes away from the Peregrine, has similarly grown alongside the Treasure Valley and is continuing to work toward a more than $40 million concourse expansion featuring up to 10 additional gates to account for the record-breaking number of enplanements observed year after year. Ahlquist has been the developer of several large-scale, mixed-use developments in recent years. In May of last year, Boise’s 4th and Idaho development held a “topping-off” ceremony to mark the progress on a two-tower, 13-story development offering for-sale condominiums. Last month, Ahlquist began the construction phase of Meridian’s The District at Ten Mile, a 200-acre development that will feature In-N-Out, Panera and Target, among others. Royce McCandless is the Statehouse reporter and covers Idaho politics. You can email him at . Sign up today for one of our great newsletters and get headlines right in your inbox every morning. Sign up now! To view our latest e-Edition, click the image on the left. Search the complete digital archives for all papers in the Pioneer News Group. Idaho Press 1618 N. Midland Blvd. Nampa, ID 83651 Phone: 208-467-9251 Email: Contact Us

entry.title: Boise Centre considers River Street expansion
entry.summary: Gov. Brad Little proclaimed July 2 as Wildland Firefighter D… BOISE — A persistent dry spell has inspired an ordinance out… The entrance to the Boise Centre is pictured in this file photo. BOISE — With land purchased and intentions to expand made clear, the Boise Centre is now facing a question — what do we build? Boise’s premiere event venue has been considering the area’s growth in its plans for an expansion and has purchased two parcels this year slated for a new facility. The first parcel was purchased in early April and is a 4-acre lot. It sits near the western corner of the 10th and River Street intersection. The second parcel, a 1.8-acre lot, was acquired at the end of April and is just across the street, east of 10th Street, according to a press release. A commercial building stands at the northeast corner of 11th and River streets in Boise, Tuesday, April 7, 2026. The Greater Boise Auditorium District purchased the 4-acre property — plus an additional 1.8-acre parcel — to serve as a stepping stone for the expansion of the Boise Centre. “We’re focused on building a solid foundation for both the district and greater downtown Boise that will withstand needs well into the future,” Greater Boise Auditorium District Board Chair Kristin Muchow said in the release. “This allows us to move forward and evaluate the best use of the land to serve our community and deliver economic impact to benefit the city.” At a Tuesday, May 26 meeting in the Boise Centre, the Greater Boise Auditorium District Board, which operates the center, heard from Johnson Consulting on options for growth. Project Manager John Fleming recapped discussions that have occurred over the past several months. A 2025 study by Johnson Consulting found that the center loses an average of 115 events annually due to a lack of available space. At a current size of 86,000 square feet in event space, the Boise Centre is limited by the number of events it is capable of hosting at once. Recommendations by Johnson Consulting include suggestions to double its current size. A consideration on the table is typically to expand an existing facility. For the Boise Centre in the middle of downtown, Fleming said the expansion is a challenge, with few available parcels nearby. The center could be expanded vertically, but the expansion would be limited. This naturally led to plans for a new facility on a secondary campus. Proposals have been narrowed down to three main options. The first option would consider constructing a ground-level, 60,000-square-foot exhibit hall as well as a second-story, 40,000-square-foot exhibit hall. A second option is to only construct a 50,000-square-foot exhibit hall. Both options would support a moderate attendance increase. Fleming noted that conventions and conferences at the Boise Centre are the event types that bring the greatest number of hotel stays. At the same time, the center could do better. “It’s not to say that by adding 50,000 square feet of exhibit space or 100,000 square feet broken across two levels you’re not achieving any growth,” Fleming said. “You’re achieving very substantial growth. It’s just, less than maybe what we see as the market opportunity.” The third option presented is a two-phase plan that would begin with constructing a 100,000-square-foot exhibit hall. In phase two, the center would also add 40,000 square feet in ballrooms and 20,000 square feet in meeting rooms. Also in discussion was the profitability of sports tourism. The board has considered building a multi-sport complex that can host multiple games at a time. “We see very frequently large exhibit halls hosting major volumes of sports tourism,” Fleming said. “We would see that as being certainly a part — if a satellite facility were to be built that was centered around an exhibit hall — certainly a part of that equation.”

entry.title: Micron milestone: Memory chip maker reaches $1 trillion market cap
entry.summary: Gov. Brad Little proclaimed July 2 as Wildland Firefighter D… BOISE — A persistent dry spell has inspired an ordinance out… Micron Technology in Boise, Monday, June 10, 2024. The semiconductor company’s stock price closed the trading day Tuesday up 19.29% to $895.88 per share, giving the semiconductor company a $1 trillion market cap. BOISE — Welcome to the trillion dollar club, Micron. Boise-headquartered Micron Technology’s stock price closed the trading day Tuesday up 19.29% to $895.88 per share, giving the semiconductor company a $1 trillion market cap. It marks a new high valuation for Micron and puts it in the ranks of the handful of trillion-dollar companies that includes Nvidia, Microsoft, Apple, Alphabet, Meta, Broadcom, Tesla, TSMC and Berkshire Hathaway. Micron closed Friday at $751 per share. Its stock price jumped in pre-market trading to open Tuesday $820.50, buoyed by an announcement from UBS that the investment bank had raised its target price for Micron to $1,625 per share — a tripling of its previous target of $535 set in April. “We believe the market will start to put a more ‘normal’ multiple on the stock and MU will continue to re-rate higher as more details emerge about the structural changes AI has driven to the entire memory complex,” UBS wrote in its analysis. The AI data center race has generated strong demand for the memory chips and storage products Micron and only a handful of others make. The demand is so strong that the company announced in December 2025 its exit from the consumer memory business. “The AI-driven growth in the data center has led to a surge in demand for memory and storage. Micron has made the difficult decision to exit the Crucial (brand) consumer business in order to improve supply and support for our larger, strategic customers in faster-growing segments,” the company said in a statement at the time. Micron is the only memory manufacturer based in the United States. Micron began in 1978 as a four-person semiconductor design company in the basement of a Boise dental office, according to a company timeline. Two years later, it broke ground on its first fabrication site in Boise. In June 2025, the company announced a $200 billion investment to expand domestic memory manufacturing and research and development, estimating the creation of 90,000 jobs. Micron’s plans include investing in two leading-edge, high-volume fabrication facilities in Idaho, which will create more than 17,000 new jobs, the company said. Sign up today for one of our great newsletters and get headlines right in your inbox every morning. Sign up now! To view our latest e-Edition, click the image on the left. Search the complete digital archives for all papers in the Pioneer News Group. Idaho Press 1618 N. Midland Blvd. Nampa, ID 83651 Phone: 208-467-9251 Email: Contact Us

entry.title: Terrace Drive-In will open this summer after all
entry.summary: Thousands attended the 60th God and Country Festival in Namp… At 19, Joshua Ritter, born in Moscow to parents Robert and S… A sign directs patrons to the concession area at the Terrace Drive-In theater, Thursday, May 21, 2026. The main screen and viewing area at Terrace Drive-In theater, Thursday, May 21, 2026. The main screen and viewing area at the Terrace Drive-In theater, Thursday, May 21, 2026. CALDWELL — The nostalgic longtime Terrace Drive-In movie theater will indeed have outdoor showings for the 2026 season and is set to open in July under new management. The concession/projection building and main screen at the Terrace Drive-In theater, Thursday, May 21, 2026. Being one of only two drive-in movie theaters in the Treasure Valley, Terrace Drive-In has become a staple for community members who look forward to the opening season each year. The drive-in, located at 4011 S. Lake Ave. in Caldwell, has been family-owned and operated for 35 years. But its history dates back to the 1950s when it was first built. It now remains one of the last few drive-in theaters in Idaho. Alice Estrada purchased the drive-in theater in 1991 and fully operated it herself until 2018. Since then, it has gone through multiple changes in operations management within the family, with a new operator ready to take charge as of late spring. Weeds grow through the pavement at the former entrance to the Terrace Drive-In theater, Thursday, May 21, 2026. The changes caused miscommunication between the former and incoming operators in March, leading to an inaccurate statement on the drive-in’s social media accounts informing the community that the drive-in was not going to be opening for the 2026 season due to incomplete renovations. Those social media posts reached hundreds of heartbroken community members who did not want to see another local drive-in’s existence disappear. Many of them offered ways to extend their support in the renovation efforts so that the drive-in could reopen. Numbered rows in the main viewing area at Terrace Drive-In theater, Thursday, May 21, 2026. As the property owner, Estrada told the Idaho Press that she wanted to apologize to the community and families who look forward to the drive-in’s opening and said the drive-in would be moving forward no matter what. “I apologize, not for something I did, but I still need to apologize because of the (misinformation) that was distributed through the internet,” Estrada said. While the drive-in remains closed and will have a shorter season than normal by opening from the middle of summer until late October, attendees can expect an uplifting experience with the new additions and renovations currently underway. The later-than-usual opening date is due to permit requirements, renovations and transition between operators, according to Dera Poynter, the drive-in’s new operator and family friend of Estrada, who also said she has been reviewing guests’ concerns and is exploring ways to improve their outdoor movie theater experience. The bright color of the concession and projection building stands out as the centerpiece of the Terrace Drive-In theater, Thursday, May 21, 2026. “The goal is to bring back the classic Terrace Drive-In experience people remember while refreshing concessions and adding some new energy,” Poynter said.

entry.title: Mini-subdivision approved in Traverse Creek Subdivision in Caldwell
entry.summary: Thousands attended the 60th God and Country Festival in Namp… At 19, Joshua Ritter, born in Moscow to parents Robert and S… Caldwell City Hall in downtown Caldwell. CALDWELL — Just over a one-acre parcel will be added to an already approved subdivision in Caldwell. At Monday’s city council meeting, the council unanimously voted to approve the annexation of a 1.07-acre parcel and a preliminary plat for the Traverse Landing Subdivision — which will add six homes to the 265 that are planned for the larger Traverse Creek Subdivision. The approved project was the first and smallest application request the council approved of the night. The approved subdivision will be located near North Ranch Business Park — an area of the city that has seen large amounts of development over recent years. Engineering consulting firm NV5, on behalf of Endurance Holdings LLC, received a green light to add an additional parcel to their existing home project. The approved parcel is located on the north side of Marble Front Road near the KCID Road intersection and will be zoned as R-2, or community residential. Traverse Landing Subdivision will be a single-family residential subdivision with six buildable lots and one common lot. It was unanimously approved by the city’s planning and zoning commission on April 8. It will fit within the applicant’s already approved larger project, Traverse Creek Subdivision, according to project representative Bonnie Layton. CBH Homes will build the Traverse Creek Subdivision after it was approved by the city council in 2024 and is expected to be coming soon, according to the homebuilder’s website, as it is being completed in phases. Traverse Creek Subdivision was approved to bring in 265 detached single-family homes and 14 townhomes, according to the city’s senior planner, Katie Wright. The six-lot subdivision will be built in phase one of the larger project’s six-phase plan. The Vallivue School District raised concerns about the subdivision, adding pressure to Skyway Elementary School on Ward Road, though it is not at capacity yet. Council Member Chris Allgood questioned Layton as to how one parcel was left behind when the Traverse Creek Subdivision was approved two years ago. “That happens at times — a property owner isn’t willing to sell at a certain point and for whatever reason their timing changes,” Layton said. “I’ve seen it happen time and again where somebody decided they are finally ready to move to someplace else or their life circumstances change.” Council Member Mike Dittenber made a motion to approve all three action items, followed by a second from council members Geoff Williams and Allgood. Mariela Esquivel-Rodriguez is a reporter for the Idaho Press focused on Caldwell and Canyon County. You can email her at Sign up today for one of our great newsletters and get headlines right in your inbox every morning. Sign up now! To view our latest e-Edition, click the image on the left. Search the complete digital archives for all papers in the Pioneer News Group.

entry.title: Gem In & Out thrives as longest-standing restaurant in Caldwell
entry.summary: The familiar sign of the Gem In & Out restaurant rises into the evening sky along Cleveland Boulevard in Caldwell, Friday, May 15, 2026. Automobile enthusiasts gather and show off their cars at the Gem In & Out restaurant in Caldwell, Friday, May 15, 2026. Automobile enthusiasts gather and show off their cars at the Gem In & Out restaurant in Caldwell, Friday, May 15, 2026. Automobile enthusiasts gather and show off their cars at the Gem In & Out restaurant in Caldwell, Friday, May 15, 2026. Automobile enthusiasts gather and show off their cars at the Gem In & Out restaurant in Caldwell, Friday, May 15, 2026. Automobile enthusiasts gather and show off their cars at the Gem In & Out restaurant in Caldwell, Friday, May 15, 2026. The familiar sign of the Gem In & Out restaurant rises into the evening sky along Cleveland Boulevard in Caldwell, Friday, May 15, 2026. CALDWELL — The city’s oldest restaurant has a secret recipe that has allowed it to remain operational for nearly six decades: unchangeable core values. Since its establishment in 1969, Gem In & Out has operated on its core values of family, community and humility to serve the Caldwell community and beyond bringing feelings of nostalgia not only with each step taken inside, but also with every bite of food from its decades-old menu. Gem In & Out sits at the gateway of Caldwell, located at 322 Cleveland Blvd., just four blocks from the city’s welcome sign with its motto “more to offer” after entering Cleveland Boulevard from State Highway 19. The bittersweet nostalgia has enabled the decades-old burger joint to become a staple for the Caldwell and classic car community. Every Friday evening from 6 p.m. to 8 p.m., Gem In & Out hosts a car meet where classic car collectors gather, adding to the restaurant’s oldies-style atmosphere, and has helped attract customers from all walks of life. Even despite operating during the country’s high economic challenges, the restaurant staff has relied on the idea that if they took care of their community, the community would take care of them. After successfully getting through tough times that other business owners may have not, former longtime owner Tim Nielsen decided it was time to hang up the gloves four years ago and pass a family-owned business to his daughter-like employee of over 30 years, Kimberly Egan. Gem In & Out was founded in 1961, then purchased by Nielsen’s parents in 1969. The name Gem represents Idaho’s nickname “The Gem State” and In & Out was used as a generic term to describe fast food. Nielsen then purchased the burger joint from his parents in 1981 when he was just 22 years old, vowing to keep the same recipes on their classic food menu items. And while the world’s technology has advanced, operations that have successfully worked remain the same at Gem In & Out, by still using an old drive-thru sound system, cash register and hand note orders. It’s a system that has been Nielsen’s golden rule over the years: “if it’s not broken, don’t fix it.” It was imperative for Nielsen that he continued the legacy of the small and independently owned burger joint his parents established, especially as numerous fast-food corporations took over many locations across the country. “I wanted it to continue and survive because the time and effort I put into it is kind of a legacy, and corporate America has taken over everything, and there is nowhere better to eat than a ma-and-pa establishment,” Nielsen said. Nielsen’s father died in 2019, but his mother remains alive and filled with pride to know the restaurant she and late husband opened is still fully operational as it was 57 years ago. There is also a far deeper importance of keeping the same legacy recipes that were established by Nielsen’s family in 1969. After someone who grew up going to Gem In & Out relocates to a new city and later returns to Caldwell, those exact flavors they taste are meant to provide deep human connection through memories from the past.

entry.title: New outdoor event venue fledges in Boise this summer
entry.summary: Thousands attended the 60th God and Country Festival in Namp… At 19, Joshua Ritter, born in Moscow to parents Robert and S… Bluebird Social, opening in June, will be located just off the Boise River, at 1401 W. Shoreline Drive on the greenbelt. BOISE — “What do you guys normally look for to play a gig like this?” Jaxson Power-Thornton, the 26-year-old co-creative director of Bluebird Social, asks over the phone as he paces around what is still just a construction site. Power-Thornton is seeking to book Lobo Lara, a local Latin fusion band, as one of this summer’s acts for Bluebird Social’s debut season. Nestled downtown along the Boise River Greenbelt, Power-Thornton’s family aims to create a boutique outdoor concert venue for their latest project. “[The Greenbelt] is such a trademark of Boise, and kind of underdeveloped in many ways in terms of social gathering spaces. Too much of that has had to go into Garden City,” said Paul Thornton, father of Jaxson Power-Thornton and founder of Bluebird Social’s parent company TAG Presents. The Thornton name first became prominent in the Treasure Valley with the launch of Bravo Entertainment in 1995. A locally based outfit that would grow into one of the most prolific promoters in the Pacific Northwest, one of Bravo’s first gigs was hosting Rage Against the Machine at the Old Idaho Penitentiary. They inaugurated what was then just known as the Idaho Center in Nampa with Shania Twain’s Come On Over tour, and opened the Big Easy Concert House a few years later. “I think my mom was pregnant with me at the Big Easy, when they were opening that thing,” Power-Thornton said. “There’s photos of me as a baby with, like, big headphones on and whatnot.” It was only fitting that Power-Thornton would grow to have a pivotal role in the family business as well. Though Bravo was sold and the Big Easy became the Knitting Factory when he was in elementary school, the Thorntons remained active in entertainment with the founding of TAG. He recalls running towels for performers at the Formula 1 track his parents opened in Austin — “I had to go and buy 500 black towels for Toby Keith at one point” — and was a student at Chapman University when the Thorntons launched the Wonderfront Music Festival in San Diego. That was where girlfriend Lyelca Rodrigues entered the picture. “I was working on another brand activation there, and [TAG] picked me up from there and hired me,” Rodrigues, 32, said. “I do everything online when it comes to creative direction and the digital space.” And lately, most of that energy has gone into promoting Bluebird Social ahead of its kickoff next month. The venue will take over 1401 W. Shoreline Drive, the space formerly occupied by Green Acres Food Truck (which is now located at 767 S. Pioneer St.). In addition to free concerts (the first with Sugarboo on June 4) and ticketed events (like Canadian indie-pop band Bombargo on June 12), the venue also plans to host food trucks for lunch and dinner daily, with Sear + Savor being a permanent fixture this summer. The original trolley from Boise Trolley Tours will be repurposed as a grab-and-go stop for drinks. A gazebo will feature books, games and a piano, in hopes of becoming Bluebird’s space for impromptu jam sessions. When artists aren’t occupying the big stage, Power-Thornton wants it to be Boise’s premier locale for watch parties. “One of our big goals is to be Boise’s destination for watching the World Cup this summer,” he said. “We’re going to be showing every single game, from the first game to the finals.” The name Bluebird Social was chosen, after hundreds of monikers were brainstormed, as a nod to Idaho’s heritage — referencing both the state bird and the ski term “bluebird day” to mean a sunny, cloudless sky. “We build a city in a week when we do music festivals, and then I watch it get torn down after three days,” Rodrigues said. “It hurts so much and the fact that I know this is going to stay is such a beautiful feeling.”

entry.title: Bargains abound with new grocer coming to town
entry.summary: Thousands attended the 60th God and Country Festival in Namp… At 19, Joshua Ritter, born in Moscow to parents Robert and S… An upcoming ribbon-cutting ceremony will ring in a new discount grocer in Kuna. Grocery Outlet will open in Kuna on Thursday, May 21, and will offer discounts and giveaways as part of the ribbon-cutting event. The store, which will be independently owned by Brandon and Julia Mansfield, will open at 10 a.m. May 21 at 1350 E. Deer Flat Road. During the event, the Mansfields will donate $500 to the Boys and Girls Club of Ada County and $500 to Kuna Food Bank. But those looking for prizes early on a Thursday morning also can get in on the action. Starting at 8 a.m., the first 100 customers will receive a Bliss Buck Gift Card worth mystery amounts of Grocery Outlet store credit, with the top prize being $500. Additionally, shoppers will receive one free reusable bag with their purchase through opening week, while supplies last. Customers can also enter to win $1,000 in free groceries from May 21 through June 19. Madison Guernsey is the News Editor at the Idaho Press. Sign up today for one of our great newsletters and get headlines right in your inbox every morning. Sign up now! To view our latest e-Edition, click the image on the left. Search the complete digital archives for all papers in the Pioneer News Group. Idaho Press 1618 N. Midland Blvd. Nampa, ID 83651 Phone: 208-467-9251 Email: Contact Us

entry.title: Two sides to one coin in Boise’s newest high-rise hotel
entry.summary: Gov. Brad Little proclaimed July 2 as Wildland Firefighter D… BOISE — A persistent dry spell has inspired an ordinance out… One of the outdoor seating areas available to guests at the new AC/Element Hotel in downtown Boise, Monday, May 11, 2026. Chicken Lollipops are served in The Boise Post Restaurant at the new AC/Element Hotel in downtown Boise, Monday, May 11, 2026. Tom Lewis, Mike Webster and Jared Smith of Pennbridge Hospitality show off a guest breakfast area during a tour of the new AC/Element Hotel in downtown Boise, Monday, May 11, 2026. BOISE — A new 15-story building has graced the downtown skyline, offering hotel stays and a restaurant as the cherry on top. The new AC/Element Hotel rises 15 stories into the skyline of downtown Boise, Monday, May 11, 2026. At 180 feet tall, Marriott Bonvoy’s newest hotel joins the ranks of Boise’s 10 tallest buildings. It brings with it 296 rooms and 260 parking spaces in its attached parking garage. Located at 1005 W. Grove St., the hotel features a two-in-one concept with an AC Hotel and Element under the same roof. The two Marriott brands have their own styles and cater to different needs. “It’s one address, but two hotels, right, both under the Marriott Bonvoy system,” Jared Smith, co-founder of Pennbridge Hospitality, said during a Monday tour of the new building. “And this happens more and more where you want two different Marriott hotels, the experience that’s offered by both and you want to put them into one building.” Pennbridge is a franchisee of the Marriott lodging brand and brought the new development to downtown. Pennbridge Hospitality co-founder Tom Lewis stands near the front desk of the new AC/Element Hotel in downtown Boise, next to a trio of bicycles available to guests, during a tour of the property, Monday, May 11, 2026. The lobby puts the two brands into contrast. The Element side of the hotel embraces natural light with lots of white and light-colored woods. High ceilings paired with floor-to-ceiling windows allow sunlight into the space. In addition, the Element brand puts an emphasis on wellness and the environment. Green bicycles in the lobby are for guests to borrow, and each Element room has filtered drinking water for guests. The AC, on the other hand, embraces modernism and moody decor. Black walls, unique art and strategically placed mirrors rule the lobby. Its minimalistic style lends to a sleek look and puts an emphasis on luxury. The Element rooms lend well to long stays. A high-end executive suite includes a large living area, kitchen and a private patio area. A double king room at the new AC/Element Hotel in downtown Boise, Monday, May 11, 2026. Throughout the hotel, there are a number of rooms with two king beds. “Based on our learnings from the residents, there is demand for double bedrooms, especially on the weekends,” said Mike Webster, president of operations for Pennbridge. “So we increased the mix.” The key distinction of this Marriott from other hotels — the view.

entry.title: From Jalisco to Idaho: Caldwell-owned tequila hits local shelves
entry.summary: Thousands attended the 60th God and Country Festival in Namp… At 19, Joshua Ritter, born in Moscow to parents Robert and S… A glass of Bone Orchard tequila is poured in the tasting room at Koenig Distillery in Caldwell, Thursday, May 7, 2026. Boxes of Bone Orchard tequila sit on pallets ready for distribution at Koenig Distillery in Caldwell, Thursday, May 7, 2026. CALDWELL — A new tequila brand that combines both Idaho and Mexico’s agriculture roots is now available for the local community to taste, making it the first from the Treasure Valley. Located in the heart of Caldwell’s Sunnyslope Wine Region, Koenig Distillery has provided craft spirits for the last 27 years — from brandy and bourbon whiskey, to potato and huckleberry vodka. Now, owner and master distiller Andy Koenig joins one of the world’s fastest-growing markets — the tequila industry. “There’s a little segment where we were just kind of missing out — and it’s that tequila segment, which is big in Idaho, and was missing from our portfolio,” Koenig said. A glass of Bone Orchard Tequila is poured in the tasting room at Koenig Distillery in Caldwell, Thursday, May 7, 2026. He recently launched his own tequila brand, Bone Orchard Tequila, just last month and officially landed a spot on the shelves in each of Idaho’s stated-owned liquor stores, and of course at his local distillery located at 20928 Grape Lane in Caldwell. Koenig is partnering with Casa Maestri, a highly awarded and family-owned distillery located in Tequila, Jalisco, Mexico, to bring Bone Orchard Tequila to life offering drinkers a glass of tequila made from 100% Blue Agave straight from the motherland. The name “Bone Orchard” refers to a western slang term that means cowboy cemetery and was chosen to incorporate an old western feel. The bottle’s logo displays a cutout depicting the Owyhee Mountains, which can be seen outside the windows of the Koenig Distillery, chosen with the intention to represent both Idaho and Mexico’s climates and mountainous landscapes. Andrew Koenig, president and master distiller at Koenig Distillery in Caldwell, discusses the recent release of Bone Orchard Tequila, Thursday, May 7, 2026. Bone Orchard Tequila’s slogan, “unearth your adventurous spirit,” which is also found on the bottle’s label, is a tribute to the fearless spirits hidden within the Owyhee Desert, and nods to the notion that “every great adventure begins with a journey into the unknown.” Koenig’s tequila brand’s slogan could also be aligned with his career journey of owning a distillery, continuously entering the unknown and not-guaranteed success of spirit production — yet he stepped into another adventure by creating his own tequila while appreciating and respecting the land and culture where it is crafted. Just like the process of creating a successful distillery, creating a tequila brand takes time and patience — something that Koenig is used to. He especially appreciates the art behind the process of producing every bottle. “That’s what interests me the most is the process and just taking something that comes off the orchard out here and then ending up in a finished bottle is kind of satisfying to see a whole process take place,” Koenig said. Andrew Koenig, president and master distiller at Koenig Distillery in Caldwell, discusses the recent release of Bone Orchard Tequila, Thursday, May 7, 2026. And while he never imagined that he would have a tequila brand of his own when he opened the distillery at 26 years old, he jumped on the idea four years ago after realizing he could do more with the thousands of barrels that are used to age the bourbon inside the warehouses that he built from the ground up. Bone Orchard Tequila is offered to customers in three different aging categories: an unaged blanco, a one-year aged reposado, and the añejo, which is aged for three years.
